lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market ophthalmic goods distributors that repeatedly provide external optometry, ophthalmology, optical-retail, institutional, or consumer-channel customers with outsourced inventory, prescription-order fulfillment, product and frame curation, tracing or recall support, returns, practice support, or equipment-related service.
lens.excluded_activities: Product-only frame or lens wholesalers without a recurring or repeat outsourced service, lens and frame manufacturers, optical laboratories classified as manufacturing, integrated retailers' captive units, eye-care practices, marketplaces, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers buy frames, lenses, contact lenses, instruments, accessories, and replenishment at distribution margins; eligible firms layer repeat fulfillment, inventory, prescription workflow, customer support, returns, traceability, curation, or equipment support onto those sales.
lens.deviation_from_default: The small code mixes commodity replenishment, fashion frames, prescription products, instruments, and product-only wholesalers. It is narrowed to firms with a material repeat or recurring outsourced-service obligation so merchant resale is not mistaken for service delivery.

a.bridge: BLS observes occupations for the broader 423400 professional and commercial equipment wholesale industry, not wage-weighted task exposure in eligible 423460 firms. I map its sales, customer-service, management, software, support, repair, and logistics mix to general task-level AI evidence, assigning high exposure to product search, quoting, prescription intake, service response, catalog work, returns, and back office but low exposure to warehousing, physical inspection, fitting-related escalation, and repair. The parent-industry mix may overstate software and medical-equipment repair relative to ophthalmic goods.
a.rationale: Ophthalmic distribution contains repetitive information work in catalog and frame management, quote and order entry, prescription verification support, inventory and replenishment, customer service, returns, supplier coordination, and account management. Physical fulfillment, quality inspection, specialized instruments, and high-cost prescription errors keep the exposed share well below a fully digital service.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task census and current-automation audit across independent 423460 distributors, separately covering frames, contacts, lenses, instruments, inside sales, warehouse, and customer support.
caveats: BLS occupation data are at 423400, not 423460.
General AI research measures exposure or use rather than economically removable labor.
The service-rich subset may have a different labor mix from the full six-digit code.

rho.rationale: Five-year implementation is plausible for order capture, prescription-data checks, catalog enrichment, product recommendations, service triage, return authorization, purchasing, inventory exceptions, customer communication, and accounting. Realization is limited by fragmented practice systems, supplier catalogs, prescription accuracy, recordkeeping, data privacy, quality control, and the economics of integrating small distributors. FTC rules also impose specific verification and three-year recordkeeping duties on contact-lens sellers.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at ophthalmic distributors showing workflow adoption, verification exceptions, prescription and fulfillment errors, service levels, labor hours removed, and sustained hiring avoidance over at least 12 months.
caveats: No representative implementation study for 423460 was found.
Contact-lens rules do not govern all ophthalmic goods.
Small firms may lack the data quality and integrations needed for high implementation.

e.rationale: The official industry is merchant wholesale rather than outsourced service, and fashion-frame or commodity-goods sellers may offer little beyond product availability. A minority nevertheless performs repeat prescription fulfillment, inventory, curation, returns, practice support, traceability, or equipment service. The narrow lens and very small frozen firm count make the estimate especially sensitive to firm classification.
e.change_evidence: A census of all frozen-band 423460 firms using service descriptions, contracts, staff roles, and revenue mix to identify material recurring or repeat outsourced-service obligations.
caveats: The frozen n is margin-bridged from broad distributor economics and is only ESTIMATE quality.
Retail filings illustrate channels and outsourced fulfillment but not the prevalence of eligible independent wholesalers.
Repeat delivery alone is insufficient without an outsourced-service component.

s5.bridge: Census measures owner age across U.S. employer businesses and demographic groups in wholesale trade, not five-year qualifying control transfers among eligible 423460 firms. I convert the older-owner stock into a substantially lower transaction probability after excluding retention, closure, family or internal succession, minority deals, and nontransferable operations. The concentrated optical supply chain may increase strategic sales but also reduce the buyer pool.
s5.rationale: Census reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were 55 or older and identifies wholesale trade as an older-owner sector. Only part of that stock will transfer control within five years, and customer, brand, laboratory, and supplier relationships determine whether an operation is transferable.
s5.change_evidence: A denominator-complete match of eligible 423460 firms to GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, optical-industry brokers, and business registrations, including sales, internal succession, and closures.
caveats: Broad and demographic-subgroup age data do not observe transaction timing.
No six-digit deal denominator was found.
With only 95 frozen-band firms, a few events materially change the rate.

q.rationale: Internal overhead and fixed service or fulfillment fees can retain benefit, but frames, contact lenses, and standard eyewear are price-visible and exposed to online, integrated, and direct channels. Warby Parker describes competition on brand, quality, price, innovation, and style; National Vision reports heavy supplier concentration, with 96% of contact-lens spend across three vendors and 86% of lens spend at one vendor. These conditions give suppliers and customers leverage and encourage renewal repricing.
q.change_evidence: Contract and account cohorts from independent distributors showing gross-profit retention after automation, customer repricing, supplier term changes, churn, and win/loss behavior by frames, contacts, lenses, instruments, and service fees.
caveats: Retailer filings are directional channel evidence, not direct measures of wholesaler benefit retention.
Fashion frames and prescribed contacts have different pricing dynamics.
Supplier rebates, exclusivity, and private-label terms can dominate operating savings.

d5.bridge: CDC and Warby Parker describe population need, market size, and purchase frequency rather than five-year constant-price demand for the current 423460 service basket. I use the high prevalence of vision correction, annual or biennial replenishment, aging-related vision burden, and recurring contact-lens purchases to anchor modest real growth, then widen for channel shifts, fashion and price cycles, and retailer or manufacturer integration. The proxy can overstate wholesale demand if direct-to-consumer and captive supply chains take share while end-user demand grows.
d5.rationale: Warby Parker reports that more than 80% of U.S. adults use vision correction, about half of glasses wearers buy annually and another third biennially, and more than 80% of contact wearers buy at least annually. CDC says aging will increase the public-health burden of vision impairment and reports 13.6% of respondents aged 65 or older had vision impairment. These support recurring quantity, but not necessarily independent wholesale channel growth.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-price 423460 forecast separating frames, spectacle lenses, contacts, instruments, and outsourced fulfillment, with unit volumes, replacement cycles, channel shares, and independent-practice counts.
caveats: Warby Parker market estimates come from an interested retailer and cover consumer eyewear rather than six-digit wholesale output.
Vision impairment prevalence is not the same as purchases of corrective goods.
Integrated labs, retailer consolidation, and direct channels can disconnect end demand from wholesaler demand.

o.rationale: Prescription validation, physical fulfillment, quality control, inventory custody, returns, and instrument service continue to require accountable organizations; FTC rules require sellers to obtain or verify prescriptions and retain specified records. However, the accountable operator need not remain an independent wholesaler: integrated optical groups, manufacturer-direct channels, online sellers, captive laboratories, and retailer distribution can absorb the same functions. National Vision already outsources contact-lens fulfillment to a third party but also operates centralized labs, illustrating both persistence and migration of operator roles.
o.change_evidence: Channel-migration cohorts from optical practices and retailers measuring current wholesale service quantity shifted to manufacturers, integrated labs, marketplaces, self-service, or retailer-owned distribution, while identifying functions that still require external accountability.
caveats: The lens selects service-bearing firms, which raises operator dependence relative to the full code.
Operator-required demand can persist while moving away from firms of the lens's kind.
Automation of prescription verification may reduce labor without eliminating seller responsibility.

narrative.executive_view: The defensible subset is a small group of ophthalmic distributors with real fulfillment, inventory, prescription-workflow, practice-support, or equipment-service obligations. Demand for vision correction is recurrent, but the independent wholesale channel faces unusually strong integrated, online, and supplier-concentrated alternatives.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve product and frame search, catalog enrichment, quote and order capture, prescription-data validation support, reorder prediction, return authorization, customer response, purchasing exceptions, and back-office reconciliation. Humans remain needed for quality control, exception resolution, physical fulfillment, instrument work, sensitive customer issues, and accountability for prescribed-product errors.
narrative.value_capture: Savings are most retainable in internal overhead and contracted fulfillment or service. Standard contacts, frames, and lenses are transparent and competitively shopped, so online sellers, integrated players, suppliers, and optical customers are likely to capture part of any productivity gain.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen LMM population is only 95 firms and includes product-only wholesalers that fail the lens. Broad owner-age evidence suggests succession potential, but the eligible count and control-transfer rate are not observed and are highly sensitive to a few firms.
narrative.demand_durability: Vision correction has high penetration and frequent replacement, while aging increases vision needs. The concern is channel durability rather than end demand: retail chains, manufacturers, captive labs, and direct-to-consumer platforms can internalize or bypass independent wholesale functions.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Principal risks include supplier concentration, brand and fashion cycles, online price competition, prescription and recordkeeping errors, inventory obsolescence, customer concentration, retailer integration, and a very small acquisition universe. Proxy evidence is much stronger on consumer eyewear need than on independent distributor service economics.
narrative.principal_driver: High-penetration, recurring vision-correction purchases create repeat information, fulfillment, and support workflows that AI can streamline.
narrative.principal_weakness: Integrated optical and direct channels can preserve end demand while eliminating the independent wholesaler from the value chain.

sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024
sources.supports: Observed largest occupations and counts for the broader NAICS 423400 industry, used to anchor the labor-mix proxy.

sources.title: GPTs are GPTs: An early look at the labor market impact potential of large language models
sources.supports: General U.S. task-exposure benchmark: about 80% of workers could have at least 10% of tasks affected and 19% could have at least 50% affected.

sources.title: The Anthropic Economic Index
sources.supports: Observed Claude task-use evidence, including 57% augmentation and 43% automation, with explicit representativeness caveats.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: 2019 Annual Business Survey graphic reporting 51% of responding employer-business owners were age 55 or older.

sources.title: The Metamorphosis of Women Business Owners: A Focus on Age
sources.supports: Census evidence that older employer owners are prominent in wholesale trade, used only as a succession proxy.

sources.title: Warby Parker Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Eyewear-market size, vision-correction penetration, replenishment frequency, contact-lens recurrence, competitive channels, independent-retailer share, and supply-chain structure.

sources.title: National Vision Holdings, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Third-party contact-lens fulfillment, centralized laboratory operations, and supplier concentration including 96% of contact-lens spend across three vendors and 86% of lens spend with one vendor.

sources.title: The Contact Lens Rule: A Guide for Prescribers and Sellers
sources.supports: Seller prescription-verification procedures, required request information, eight-business-hour response framework, prohibited practices, and three-year recordkeeping.

sources.title: Vision Impairment and Chronic Health Conditions
sources.supports: CDC finding that 13.6% of national survey respondents age 65 or older reported vision impairment and that aging is expected to increase the public-health burden.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 423460 Ophthalmic Goods Merchant Wholesalers
sources.supports: Official industry classification scope anchoring the merchant-wholesale population and narrower service-bearing lens.

sources.title: Taking Care of Your Eyes
sources.supports: CDC evidence that refractive errors are common, an estimated 11 million Americans age 12 or older could see better with corrective lenses or surgery, and older adults have greater vision needs.
